Polity: Northern Yan / 北燕（十六国）
Notes: Regional Murong-led state with intermittent stability and constrained long-term capacity.

Polity: Song / 宋
Notes: Reestablishment of southern court authority and administrative consolidation define the high phase.

Polity: Song / 宋
Notes: Factional struggles and military pressures eroded central control leading toward dynastic change.

Polity: Gaochang / 高昌
Notes: Local dynasty rule stabilized the Turpan oasis and integrated Silk Road commerce.

Polity: Gaochang / 高昌
Notes: Peak political stability and commercial prosperity under the Qu ruling house.

Polity: Gaochang / 高昌
Notes: Increasing Tang pressure reduced autonomy and culminated in annexation.

Polity: Southern Qi / 南齐
Notes: Xiao Daocheng established the regime and maintained relative administrative continuity.

Polity: Southern Qi / 南齐
Notes: Intense court factionalism and coups weakened the state before Liang takeover.

Polity: Liang / 梁
Notes: Xiao Yan consolidated rule and restored effective southern governance.

Polity: Liang / 梁
Notes: Strong economy and major Buddhist cultural patronage marked the dynasty’s height.

Polity: Liang / 梁
Notes: Devastating rebellion destroyed central authority and fragmented the regime.

Polity: Eastern Wei / 东魏
Notes: Effective military governance maintained northern territorial stability.

Polity: Eastern Wei / 东魏
Notes: Gao family consolidation culminated in formal dynastic replacement.

Polity: Western Wei / 西魏
Notes: Military-administrative restructuring strengthened governance and aristocratic integration.

Polity: Western Wei / 西魏
Notes: Political authority consolidated under the Yuwen clan prior to dynastic change.

Polity: Northern Qi / 北齐
Notes: Early Gao rulers maintained strong military capacity and administrative control.

Polity: Northern Qi / 北齐
Notes: Internal corruption and erratic leadership weakened the state before Zhou conquest.

Polity: Western Liang / 西梁
Notes: Small Liang successor state surviving under Northern Zhou and Sui protection.

Polity: Northern Zhou / 北周
Notes: Regime consolidation and military reforms strengthened the northern state.

Polity: Northern Zhou / 北周
Notes: Successful conquest of Northern Qi created northern hegemony before Sui takeover.

Polity: Chen / 陈
Notes: Chen Baxian restored order in the south with moderate economic recovery.

Polity: Chen / 陈
Notes: Limited military capacity left the state vulnerable to Sui reunification campaigns.

Polity: Sui / 隋
Notes: Centralized reforms and canal construction established imperial structures and fiscal capacity.

Polity: Sui / 隋
Notes: Massive military campaigns and grand projects overstretched resources and precipitated rapid collapse.

Polity: Eastern Turkic Khaganate / 东突厥汗国
Notes: Confederation-building and steppe military cohesion created a durable nomadic polity.

Polity: Eastern Turkic Khaganate / 东突厥汗国
Notes: Peak diplomatic-military influence over northern China followed by defeat and collapse.

Polity: Tang / 唐
Notes: Li Yuan and early reforms re-established imperial order and institutional foundations.

Polity: Tang / 唐
Notes: Territorial expansion, economic prosperity, and cultural-technological florescence mark the dynasty’s peak.

Polity: Tang / 唐
Notes: Post-rebellion restoration of central authority and renewed cultural production, but with limits.

Polity: Tang / 唐
Notes: Political factionalism, regional militarization, and fiscal stress produced terminal decline.

Polity: Tibetian Empire / 吐蕃
Notes: Unification of Tibetan polities and sustained expansion established a powerful plateau state.

Polity: Tibetian Empire / 吐蕃
Notes: Golden-age consolidation, religious patronage, and large-scale polity reach mark the high period.

Polity: Tibetian Empire / 吐蕃
Notes: Internal divisions and external pressure led to political fragmentation and loss of cohesion.

Polity: Balhae / 渤海
Notes: State formation linking former Goguryeo elites with frontier trade routes and agrarian bases.

Polity: Balhae / 渤海
Notes: Economic integration, diplomatic ties, and cultural patronage mark the polity’s high point.

Polity: Balhae / 渤海
Notes: Internal fragmentation and rising external pressures reduced sovereignty and led to collapse.

Polity: Japan (Nara-Heian Period) / 日本（奈良-平安时代）
Notes: Imperial codification and Buddhist-state patronage created early centralized institutions.

Polity: Japan (Nara-Heian Period) / 日本（奈良-平安时代）
Phase Name: Heian Peak: Aristocratic Culture and Stability
Notes: Fujiwara hegemony, court culture, and economic stability mark the polity’s apex.

Polity: Japan (Nara-Heian Period) / 日本（奈良-平安时代）
Notes: Provincial militarization and warrior ascendancy culminated in the Gempei wars and regime change.
